name: Perry
short_description: Coastal Maine rockhounding near the Bold Coast. Search for tourmaline, feldspar, and mica in pegmatite outcrops and glacial deposits along this scenic eastern Maine location.
description:
Perry sits in Washington County, Maine, the easternmost county in the United States, positioned within the Acadian geological province that dominates eastern Maine. This region is famous for its pegmatite formations and mineral-rich metamorphic rocks, particularly the tourmaline and feldspar deposits found throughout Washington County. The flat terrain at 14 feet elevation is characteristic of Maine's coastal plain, where Pleistocene glaciation shaped the modern landscape and exposed mineral-bearing bedrock.

The primary rockhounding opportunities here involve searching pegmatite outcrops and glacial erratics that contain tourmaline, feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens. Washington County pegmatites formed during the Devonian to Carboniferous periods when magmatic activity intruded into the metamorphic host rocks. Glacial activity during the last ice age (Pleistocene) scoured the landscape and carried erratic blocks containing tourmaline and other pegmatite minerals, depositing them across the coastal plain where they can still be found today.

The relatively flat, accessible terrain makes this location suitable for rockhounders of various skill levels. Local roadsides, abandoned quarries (where legally accessible), and natural exposures offer collecting opportunities. The proximity to the coast and Bold Coast area adds scenic value to your rockhounding visit. Be aware that some areas may be private property, so always confirm access before collecting.

Best results come from examining pegmatite exposures for terminating tourmaline crystals (commonly black, but sometimes colored), tabular feldspar (often pink or white), and well-formed quartz. The glacial deposits scattered throughout the region can yield loose specimens and fragments of pegmatite minerals without requiring excavation.
history: Perry lies within the eastern Maine pegmatite belt, a region of Devonian-age granitic intrusions that crystallized into pegmatite bodies rich in tourmaline, feldspar, and other minerals. Glaciation during the Pleistocene epoch transported these pegmatite fragments across the landscape, making Washington County one of the most mineralogically interesting regions in New England. Historical mineral collecting in Maine has focused on pegmatite deposits for over a century, with tourmaline being the signature Maine gem mineral.
tips: Search along roadcuts, stream banks, and old quarry dumps where pegmatite has been naturally or historically exposed; tourmaline specimens often appear as dark, shiny hexagonal crystals. Bring a magnifying glass to examine feldspar for color and clarity, and look for quartz clusters in pegmatite fractures. The flat coastal terrain means you can cover more ground than in mountainous areas, so focus on exposed bedrock and glacial erratics rather than deep digging. A small chisel and hammer can help collect specimens from pegmatite outcrops, but never trespass on private property or collect in restricted areas.
terrain_notes: Very flat terrain at 14 feet elevation typical of Maine's coastal plain. Footing is generally easy, with a mix of grass, exposed bedrock outcrops, and glacial boulders. Walking distances from parking are typically short (under 1 mile to most collecting areas). Vegetation includes coastal scrub and occasional wooded patches. Roads in the area are paved and well-maintained, with gentle grades. The flat landscape means minimal elevation gain, making this accessible to most hikers.
best_season: May - September. Spring through early fall offers the best weather for rockhounding in coastal Maine. Summer months provide the most stable conditions with minimal rain and warmer temperatures (60-75F), ideal for spending extended time in the field. May and September are slightly cooler but less crowded. Winter and early spring bring frequent storms, cold temperatures, and muddy conditions that make field work difficult.
written_directions: From Perry town center, head north on State Route 190 toward Eastport. The Perry area straddles Route 190 as it passes through town. Look for exposed roadcuts and glacial boulders along Route 190 and nearby side roads. Key collecting areas are typically within 2-3 miles of Perry proper along Route 190 and local roads branching east and west. Park safely off the road at pullouts near visible pegmatite outcrops or glacial erratics. Always confirm you have permission to collect on any private land before proceeding.
road_conditions: Paved state route (Route 190) and local paved roads. Well-maintained, no high-clearance vehicle required. Gentle grades, accessible year-round when roads are passable.
parking_notes: Park at safe pullouts along Route 190 or local side roads near exposed bedrock or glacial deposits. Informal parking; no designated lot. Limited capacity at most pullouts. Always park safely off the travel lane.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Collect specimens from public roadsides and accessible natural outcrops. Do not trespass on private property. Do not collect in posted areas, state parks, or protected lands. No motorized excavation. Keep disturbance minimal. Respect all no-trespassing signs.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
Respect private property - do not trespass
Do not collect in restricted or posted areas
Minimal excavation - surface specimens preferred
No motorized equipment
nearest_services: Perry village has basic services. Eastport (approximately 8 miles north on Route 190) offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Calais (approximately 30 miles west) has full services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Paved roads to parking areas. Short walks (typically under 1 mile) to collecting sites. No significant elevation gain. Minimal muddy or slippery sections in dry weather. Sturdy footwear recommended for rocky outcrops. Generally accessible to people with limited mobility, though some roadcuts may have uneven surfaces.

faq.question: What tourmaline and feldspar specimens can I find rockhounding near Perry, Maine?
faq.answer: Perry area rockhounders commonly find black tourmaline (schorl) in pegmatite outcrops, often as well-terminated hexagonal crystals. Feldspar specimens include pink microcline and white to gray plagioclase, typically found in pegmatite in tabular form. Quartz and mica are also common. Specimens range from small fragments to hand-sized pieces depending on the exposure. Best finds come from fresh pegmatite outcrops along Route 190 and local roadcuts.

faq.question: Is rockhounding in Perry Maine free or are there fees?
faq.answer: Rockhounding in Perry is free. This is public roadside and natural area collecting with no permit fees or entrance costs. Simply park safely along Route 190 or side roads and collect specimens from accessible outcrops and glacial deposits. Always respect private property and do not collect on posted land.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding in Perry Maine?
faq.answer: May through September is best for Perry rockhounding. Summer months (June-August) offer the most stable weather with temperatures 60-75F and minimal rain. Spring and early fall are cooler but pleasant. Winter storms and muddy conditions make field work difficult from November through April. Plan your trip for dry weather when roads are clear and terrain is accessible.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks and minerals in Perry, Maine?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding in Perry. You can collect specimens from public roadsides and accessible natural outcrops without advance permission. However, always confirm you have access on any land where you plan to collect. Never trespass on private property or collect in posted areas, state parks, or protected lands.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for rockhounding near Perry?
faq.answer: Bring a small chisel and hammer for carefully extracting specimens from pegmatite outcrops. A magnifying glass helps identify feldspar and mica. Bring a collection bag or backpack for specimens. Sturdy shoes or boots are recommended for rocky terrain. A geological guidebook to Maine minerals is helpful for identification. Water and sun protection are essential, especially during summer months.

faq.question: Is Perry Maine a good rockhounding location for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Perry is excellent for beginner rockhounders. The flat terrain and easy access from Route 190 require no technical climbing or difficult hiking. Pegmatite outcrops are easy to identify, and tourmaline and feldspar are relatively straightforward to spot. The area is family-friendly with short walks to collecting sites. No permit or experience is required, making this an ideal Maine rockhounding destination for first-time collectors.

meta_title: Perry Maine Rockhounding - Find Tourmaline & Feldspar
meta_description: Rockhound tourmaline, feldspar, and quartz in Perry, Maine. Free public collecting near Eastport. Flat coastal terrain, easy access. Guide and tips included.
